subject: Science

unit.unitTitle: What Do Animals and Plants Need to Live?
unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! Welcome to your first Science unit of Year 2. My name is your AI Tutor, and I will be with you every step of the way. In this unit you are going to become a nature detective. A detective looks closely, asks good questions, and writes down clues. That is exactly what scientists do too.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 2 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of the things on this list. Read them now so you know what we are aiming for. At the end you can come back and tick each one when you can do it.
Name the things that all animals need to live: food, water, air, and shelter.
Explain what plants need to live: water, air, light from the Sun, space, and soil for most plants.
Describe different environments such as wet and dry places, sunny and shady places, and say which living things are found there.
Find and name minibeasts like ants, beetles, spiders, worms, and snails, and describe how they help nature.
Give examples of how people can harm the environment (litter and pollution) and how people can protect it.
Observe and record the weather using simple words and charts, and describe how weather affects animals and plants.
Talk about extreme weather such as drought, floods, and dust storms, and explain simple ways to stay safe.
Collect data, tally it, and make a simple bar chart, then read your chart to answer questions.
Use new science words correctly when you speak and write, like habitat, environment, and adaptation.

concepts.title: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! Welcome to your first…
concepts.explanation: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! Welcome to your first Science unit of Year 2. My name is your AI Tutor, and I will be with you every step of the way. In this unit you are going to become a nature detective. A detective looks closely, asks good questions, and writes down clues. That is exactly what scientists do too.
concepts.example: The big question for this unit is simple but very important: what do animals and plants need to live? Everywhere around you there is life. A goat chewing grass, a gecko on the wall, an ant carrying a crumb, a mango tree…

concepts.title: The big question for this unit is simple but very…
concepts.explanation: The big question for this unit is simple but very important: what do animals and plants need to live? Everywhere around you there is life. A goat chewing grass, a gecko on the wall, an ant carrying a crumb, a mango tree in the yard, and even the little green plant growing between the stones - they are all living things. And every living thing needs certain things to stay alive and healthy.
concepts.example: Think about your own family. To stay healthy, you need food to eat, clean water to drink, air to breathe, and a safe home to sleep in. Animals and plants are the same. They also have needs. When they get what they need,…

concepts.title: Think about your own family. To stay healthy, you need food…
concepts.explanation: Think about your own family. To stay healthy, you need food to eat, clean water to drink, air to breathe, and a safe home to sleep in. Animals and plants are the same. They also have needs. When they get what they need, they grow strong. When they do not, they become weak and can even die. In this unit you will learn what those needs are, and you will see how clever living things are at finding them.
concepts.example: You live in a beautiful part of the world. Whether your home is near the busy streets of Mogadishu or Hargeisa, close to the Indian Ocean coast at Mombasa or Berbera, beside the Jubba River, or out on the wide savanna,…

concepts.title: You live in a beautiful part of the world. Whether your…
concepts.explanation: You live in a beautiful part of the world. Whether your home is near the busy streets of Mogadishu or Hargeisa, close to the Indian Ocean coast at Mombasa or Berbera, beside the Jubba River, or out on the wide savanna, you are surrounded by amazing plants and animals. Camels, goats, cattle, and donkeys graze on the land. Lions, elephants, and giraffes roam the grasslands. Flamingos stand in the shallow lakes, and colourful fish swim among the coral reefs. This unit will help you understand the living world right…
concepts.example: We will explore five big ideas together. First, we will look at environments - the different places where living things make their homes. Second, we will hunt for minibeasts, the tiny animals that are all around us.…

concepts.title: We will explore five big ideas together. First, we will…
concepts.explanation: We will explore five big ideas together. First, we will look at environments - the different places where living things make their homes. Second, we will hunt for minibeasts, the tiny animals that are all around us. Third, we will learn how to protect the environment and care for the earth, which Allah has trusted us to look after. Fourth, we will watch the weather and learn to record it like real weather scientists. And fifth, we will study extreme weather such as drought and floods, and learn how to stay safe.…
concepts.example: [Fact] Did You Know? There are more living things on Earth than there are stars we can see in the night sky. Under a single footstep of soil, hundreds of tiny minibeasts may be living and working - and most of them are…

concepts.title: [Fact] Did You Know? There are more living things on Earth…
concepts.explanation: [Fact] Did You Know? There are more living things on Earth than there are stars we can see in the night sky. Under a single footstep of soil, hundreds of tiny minibeasts may be living and working - and most of them are helping the plants around them grow!

explorations.title: What Do Seeds Need to Grow?
explorations.context: To find out whether seeds need water and light to grow well. Your Hypothesis Before you start, write down what you think will happen. Which seeds do you think will grow best - the ones with water and light, or the ones without? Guessing first is what real…
explorations.prompt: Which cup grew the best? Which grew the worst?
explorations.answer: Which cup grew the best? Which grew the worst? What happened to the seeds that got no water? The seeds in the dark cupboard - were they green and strong, or pale and weak? Why do you think so? What does this investigation teach you about what plants need?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out whether seeds need water and light to grow well. Your Hypothesis Before you start, write down what you think will happen. Which seeds do you think will grow best - the ones with water and light, or the ones without? Guessing first is what real…

explorations.title: A Minibeast Hunt and Count
explorations.context: To find out which minibeasts live near your home, and which one is the most common. Your Hypothesis Which minibeast do you think you will see the most? Ants? Beetles? Write your guess before you begin.
explorations.prompt: Which minibeast did you see the most? Which did you see the least?
explorations.answer: Which minibeast did you see the most? Which did you see the least? Where did you find the most minibeasts - in the sun or in the shade? Wet or dry? Draw a bar chart of your totals. Which bar is tallest? Pick one minibeast and say what helpful job it does in nature.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which minibeasts live near your home, and which one is the most common. Your Hypothesis Which minibeast do you think you will see the most? Ants? Beetles? Write your guess before you begin.

explorations.title: Build a Weather Station
explorations.context: To observe and record the weather for one week, like a real meteorologist. Your Hypothesis What kind of weather do you think will happen most this week? Sunny? Cloudy? Write your guess.
explorations.prompt: Count up your week. How many days were sunny? How many were cloudy? How many were rainy?
explorations.answer: Count up your week. How many days were sunny? How many were cloudy? How many were rainy? Make a tally and a bar chart of the sky data. Which weather happened most? On the windy days, how did the wind flag move? Fast or slow? How do you think this week's weather affected the plants and animals near…
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To observe and record the weather for one week, like a real meteorologist. Your Hypothesis What kind of weather do you think will happen most this week? Sunny? Cloudy? Write your guess.

explorations.title: Comparing Wet and Dry Homes
explorations.context: To compare a damp, shady place and a dry, sunny place, and see which living things like each one. Your Hypothesis Do you think more little living things will be found in the damp shady place or the dry sunny place? Write your guess.
explorations.prompt: Which place had more living things? Were you surprised?
explorations.answer: Which place had more living things? Were you surprised? Which minibeasts liked the damp shade? Which liked the dry sun? Why do you think some creatures choose damp places and others choose dry, sunny places? How does this show that different living things need different environments?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To compare a damp, shady place and a dry, sunny place, and see which living things like each one. Your Hypothesis Do you think more little living things will be found in the damp shady place or the dry sunny place? Write your guess.

methods.title: What Do Seeds Need to Grow?
methods.example: To find out whether seeds need water and light to grow well. Your Hypothesis Before you start, write down what you think will happen. Which seeds do you think will grow best - the ones with water and light, or the ones without? Guessing first is what real…
steps: Put a little damp cotton wool or soil in each of the 3 cups.
Place 2 or 3 bean seeds in each cup.
Cup A: give water and put it on a sunny windowsill.
Cup B: give water but put it in a dark cupboard.
Cup C: give NO water and put it on the sunny windowsill.

methods.title: A Minibeast Hunt and Count
methods.example: To find out which minibeasts live near your home, and which one is the most common. Your Hypothesis Which minibeast do you think you will see the most? Ants? Beetles? Write your guess before you begin.
steps: Choose a safe spot outdoors - a corner of the yard, near a wall, or under a stone or leaf.
Look quietly for 10 minutes. Move slowly so you do not scare the minibeasts.
Each time you see a minibeast, make a tally mark next to its name in your table.
Gently look at one or two closely in your clear jar, then let them go back where you found them.
Count how many legs each one has to help you name it.

methods.title: Build a Weather Station
methods.example: To observe and record the weather for one week, like a real meteorologist. Your Hypothesis What kind of weather do you think will happen most this week? Sunny? Cloudy? Write your guess.
steps: Set your rain jar outside in an open spot, away from walls and trees.
Tie your wind flag to a stick and push it into the ground where the wind can reach it.
At the same time each day (for example, after fajr or midday), look at the sky and your tools.
Write down: Is it sunny, cloudy, or rainy? Is it windy or still? Is it hot or cool? Is there any rain in the jar?
Do this every day for one week.

methods.title: Comparing Wet and Dry Homes
methods.example: To compare a damp, shady place and a dry, sunny place, and see which living things like each one. Your Hypothesis Do you think more little living things will be found in the damp shady place or the dry sunny place? Write your guess.
steps: Choose your two places: one damp and shady, one dry and sunny.
Look carefully at the first place for 5 minutes. Write down every plant and minibeast you can see.
Feel the ground gently (then wash your hands). Is it damp or dry? Is the air cool or warm?
Do exactly the same at the second place.
Compare your two lists.

workedExamples.title: Counting and Charting Minibeasts
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through an example together, step by step, just like a real scientist would. Imagine that Amina went into her yard in Hargeisa with a notebook. She wanted to find out which minibeast was the most common. Here is exactly what she did.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Ask a question. Amina asked: Which minibeast will I see most in my yard in ten minutes? Step 2 - Make a plan. She decided to watch for ten minutes and make a tally mark for every minibeast she saw. Step 3 - Collect the data. She saw 6 ants, 3 beetles, 2 butterflies, and 1 spider. She made a tally mark for each one as she saw it. Step 4 - Count the tallies. Ants: 6. Beetles: 3. Butterflies: 2. Spider: 1. In total she saw 12 minibeasts.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: What do ALL animals need to live? A) toys B) food, water, air, and shelter C) televisions D) shoes
workedExamples.solution: B - Plants make their own food in their leaves using sunlight, water, and air. This is special to plants.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: What do plants use to make their own food? A) meat B) sunlight, water, and air C) sand only D) plastic
workedExamples.solution: B - An insect has 6 legs. This is a key clue for naming a minibeast.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: How many legs does an insect have? A) 4 B) 6 C) 8 D) 10
workedExamples.solution: C - A spider has 8 legs, which is why it is not an insect.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: How many legs does a spider have? A) 6 B) 2 C) 8 D) 0
workedExamples.solution: C - An ant is a small animal with no bones, so it is a minibeast. Lions, camels, and goats are large animals with bones.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a minibeast? A) a lion B) a camel C) an ant D) a goat
workedExamples.solution: B - A habitat is the home where an animal or plant lives and finds what it needs.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: What is a habitat? A) a kind of food B) the home where an animal lives C) a type of weather D) a colour
workedExamples.solution: C - Plastic rubbish in the sea is pollution because it harms living things. Rain and fresh air are natural and helpful.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is pollution? A) a growing tree B) clean rain C) plastic rubbish in the sea D) fresh air
workedExamples.solution: B - A drought is a long time with no rain, which dries up the land.

activities.title: What Do Seeds Need to Grow?
activities.materials: A few bean seeds or maize seeds (dried beans from the kitchen work well); 3 small cups, tins, or cut-open plastic bottles; Cotton wool, or a little soil; Water; A sunny windowsill and a dark cupboard
steps: Put a little damp cotton wool or soil in each of the 3 cups.
Place 2 or 3 bean seeds in each cup.
Cup A: give water and put it on a sunny windowsill.
Cup B: give water but put it in a dark cupboard.
Cup C: give NO water and put it on the sunny windowsill.

activities.title: A Minibeast Hunt and Count
activities.materials: A notebook and pencil; An old clear jar or cup (to look closely, then release the minibeast); A magnifying glass if you have one (a drop of water on clear plastic also magnifies); A soft spoon or leaf to gently lift a minibeast
steps: Choose a safe spot outdoors - a corner of the yard, near a wall, or under a stone or leaf.
Look quietly for 10 minutes. Move slowly so you do not scare the minibeasts.
Each time you see a minibeast, make a tally mark next to its name in your table.
Gently look at one or two closely in your clear jar, then let them go back where you found them.
Count how many legs each one has to help you name it.

activities.title: Build a Weather Station
activities.materials: A notebook or a chart you draw yourself; An empty clear bottle or jar to catch rain (a simple rain gauge); A strip of cloth or a light plastic bag tied to a stick (a wind flag); Your own eyes to watch the sky
steps: Set your rain jar outside in an open spot, away from walls and trees.
Tie your wind flag to a stick and push it into the ground where the wind can reach it.
At the same time each day (for example, after fajr or midday), look at the sky and your tools.
Write down: Is it sunny, cloudy, or rainy? Is it windy or still? Is it hot or cool? Is there any rain in the jar?
Do this every day for one week.

activities.title: Comparing Wet and Dry Homes
activities.materials: Your notebook and pencil; A magnifying glass if you have one; Two places to compare: a damp, shady spot (near a tap or under a plant) and a dry, sunny spot
steps: Choose your two places: one damp and shady, one dry and sunny.
Look carefully at the first place for 5 minutes. Write down every plant and minibeast you can see.
Feel the ground gently (then wash your hands). Is it damp or dry? Is the air cool or warm?
Do exactly the same at the second place.
Compare your two lists.

terms: Living thing
Something that is alive; it grows, needs food and water, and can make more of itself.
Environment
All the things around a living thing: air, water, soil, weather, and other living things.
Habitat
The home where a plant or animal lives and finds everything it needs.
Needs
Things a living thing must have to stay alive.
Shelter
A safe place where an animal rests and is protected from weather and danger.
Minibeast
A small animal with no bones, such as an insect, spider, worm, or snail.
Insect
A minibeast with 6 legs, like an ant, beetle, or butterfly.
Adaptation
A special body part or habit that helps a living thing survive where it lives.
Pollution
Harmful things people put into the air, water, or land.
Weather
What the air is like today: sunny, cloudy, rainy, windy, hot, or cool.
Drought
A long time with too little rain.
Flood
When too much rain covers the land with water.
commonMistakes: Saying plants do not need air.
Plants get their food only from soil.
The world is better without minibeasts.
They are the same kind of problem.

realProblems.prompt: These questions ask you to use your learning in real situations from your own world.
realProblems.answer: Halima must make sure the goats get water and food (grazing or fodder). Explanation: In the dry season these are hardest to find, and without them the goats become weak. Shelter from the hot sun also helps.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Halima must make sure the goats get water and food (grazing or fodder). Explanation: In the dry season these are hardest to find, and without them the goats become weak. Shelter from the hot sun also helps.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's family is travelling across dry land with their goats in the dry season. What two things must Halima make sure the goats get so they stay healthy? Explain why.
realProblems.answer: Omar should pick up the plastic bag safely and put it in a bin. Explanation: A sea turtle can mistake a floating bag for a jellyfish and eat it, which can make it very sick. Removing litter protects sea animals.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Omar should pick up the plastic bag safely and put it in a bin. Explanation: A sea turtle can mistake a floating bag for a jellyfish and eat it, which can make it very sick. Removing litter protects sea animals.

realProblems.prompt: Omar sees a plastic bag blowing near the beach at Lamu. He knows a sea turtle might eat it by mistake. What should Omar do, and why does it matter?
realProblems.answer: The plants grow fast because the rain gives them the water they were missing during the dry season. Explanation: With water, air, and sunlight all present, plants can make food and grow quickly.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The plants grow fast because the rain gives them the water they were missing during the dry season. Explanation: With water, air, and sunlight all present, plants can make food and grow quickly.

realProblems.prompt: During the Gu rains, Zahra notices the brown land turning green very quickly. Explain why the plants grow so fast when the rain comes.
realProblems.answer: Ibrahim should: (1) ask a question, (2) plan to watch each corner for the same amount of time, (3) tally the ants he sees in each corner, (4) count the tallies, (5) make a bar chart, and (6) read the chart to find the corner with the most ants. Explanation: These are the steps scientists use to…

realProblems.prompt: Ibrahim wants to find out which corner of his yard has the most ants. Describe the steps he should follow to collect and show his data.
realProblems.answer: Any two of: go indoors, close the windows and doors, and cover the nose and mouth with a cloth. Explanation: Dust storms make it hard to see and breathe, so staying inside and covering the face keeps you safe.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four things that all animals need to live.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Food, water, air, and shelter. Explanation: These four needs are true for every animal, from an ant to an elephant.
Plants need water to stay alive, to grow, and to help make their own food. Explanation: Roots drink water from the soil and carry it up to the leaves.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between an insect and a spider?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An insect has 6 legs and a spider has 8 legs. Explanation: Counting legs is the quickest way to tell them apart; a spider is not an insect.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two helpful jobs that minibeasts do in nature.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any two of: carrying pollen between flowers (pollination), breaking down dead leaves and dung to make soil, cleaning up scraps, and being food for other animals. Explanation: Minibeasts do small but very important jobs.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example of how people can harm the environment.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any one of: dropping litter, polluting water or air, or cutting down too many trees. Explanation: These actions take away the things animals and plants need.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Give one example of how you can protect the environment.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Any one of: not dropping litter, saving water, reusing and recycling, planting trees, or being kind to animals. Explanation: Small good actions help care for the earth.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of the things on this list. Read them now so you know what we are aiming for. At the end you can come back and tick each one when you can do it.
I can name the things that all animals need to live: food, water, air, and shelter.
I can explain what plants need to live: water, air, light from the Sun, space, and soil for most plants.
I can describe different environments such as wet and dry places, sunny and shady places, and say which living things are found there.
I can find and name minibeasts like ants, beetles, spiders, worms, and snails, and describe how they help nature.
I can give examples of how people can harm the environment (litter and pollution) and how people can protect it.
I can observe and record the weather using simple words and charts, and describe how weather affects animals and plants.
I can talk about extreme weather such as drought, floods, and dust storms, and explain simple ways to stay safe.
